pages.raw_text:
Special Correspondence to The Press


COLUMBUS, o., Oct. 13.-Ohio
is too much surprised and excited
over the saloon question to pay
much attention to the presidential
race. The latter is a question for
November. The saloon issue con-
fronts voters constantly and elec-
lions are held daily to decide
whether they shall go or stay.


The last legislature passed a
county local option bill that went
into effect a few weeks ago. 1t
was the plan of the Anti-saloon
league, led by Superintendent
Wayne B. Wheeler, to have imme-
date elections in 20 counties that
were pretty certain to go dry. Then
the fight was to be prosecuted
again after the November election.
But he was wrong in thinking that
the enemies of the saloon could be
held in restraint. From counties in
all parts of the state came demands
that the league lead them in cleo
ton contests. The league was busy
and begged them to wait. Their
answer was that they would attend
to the matter themselves. AS a
result the league is leading in a
fight twice as big as it had mapped
out. And with startling results.


Counties began going dry at the
rate of one day. A month after
the opening of the battle the drys
had won in 80 of the 81 counties.
In their single victory the vets
saved "a saloons. In their 30 vic
tories the drys wiped out S72
saloons. The saloon tax in this
state is $1,000 year. The drys
have thus cut off public revenue
to the amount of $S72,000, but they
feel that the result Is worth the
cost


1t is freely predicted that by the
middle of November more than half
the SO counties of the state will be
dry. Otto Horix, secretary of the
Liberty league, the organization OPP
posing prohibition, comedies that
the drys will be able to carry 60
counties. Even If his estimate is
correct the saloons in the wet coun-
ties are in grave danger.


In 80 counties the drys have had
just short of 10000 majority. The
fights in the next 30 will be some
what closer, but it is estimated that
they will swell the dry majority to
over 10000. The drys will then be
In position to demand state pro-
hibition and they figure that even
II the vets should retain 2S coun-
ties they could not. in a state vote
overcome the 10000 majority III the
dry counties.
